title: Sampling
summary: 9609 AS — sampling methods, sample size, representativeness, and bias.
learningObjectives: Explain why sampling is used in market research.
Describe random, stratified, and quota sampling.
Recommend sampling approach for case scenarios.

simpleExplanation.title: Asking the right people
simpleExplanation.summary: Sampling selects a subset of the population to survey — faster and cheaper than a census, but only valid if the sample represents the target market.
steps: Random: every member has equal selection chance.
Quota: fill categories to match population profile.

flashcards.back: Cheaper and faster than surveying entire population; feasible for primary research.
Every member of population has equal chance of selection — reduces selection bias.
Population divided into strata (e.g. age groups); random sample from each in proportion.
Interviewers fill quotas (e.g. 50 male, 50 female) to match profile — not random within quota.
Sample does not reflect population — results cannot be generalised.
Usually more reliable (lower sampling error) but higher cost.

sections.content: **Sampling** (3.2.3) follows **3.2.2**. When a case says "surveyed 200 people in one city", ask: **Is the sample representative of the target market?**

**Random** — names from database, lottery selection; best for statistical validity.

**Stratified** — ensure age/income/region proportions match population; good for **segmented markets**.

**Quota** — quick street interviews filling quotas; **risk of interviewer bias** choosing approachable people.

sections.question: A skincare brand targets women aged 25–40 nationwide. A student surveys 50 classmates. Evaluate the sampling.
sections.solution:
**Highly biased / unrepresentative.**

**Problems:** Wrong **age profile** (likely 16–18); **geographic** cluster (one school); **convenience sample** not random or stratified.

**Improvement:** **Stratified random sample** of women 25–40 across regions; online panel or random phone/email from customer database; minimum **200+** for basic reliability.

Invalid sample → **wrong product/pricing decisions** — wasted marketing spend.

sections.content: Always name the **bias** (age, location, income, self-selection) when criticising case research.
